memes.label: "Augustus Momson"
memes.description: "Young student at Bowick School, nephew of Mrs. Momson, known as 'dear little Gus' and the subject of anxious parental concern during the Peacocke scandal."

memes.label: "The Bishop of Broughton"
memes.description: "The Bishop of Broughton, a Church of England diocesan who investigates the scandal surrounding Mr. Peacocke's marriage and becomes an institutional antagonist to Dr. Wortle."

memes.label: "The Bishop's Investigation"
memes.description: "The Bishop of Broughton's investigation into the scandal at Bowick School represents the institutional response to the Peacocke affair and becomes a test of ecclesiastical authority versus individual conscience."

memes.label: "Bowick"
memes.description: "The village in the English countryside where Dr. Wortle's school and rectory are located, serving as the primary setting for the novel."

memes.label: "Bowick Church"
memes.description: "The parish church of Bowick where Dr. Wortle preaches as rector and where Mr. Peacocke's right to officiate becomes the public face of the scandal, representing the intersection of private conscience and public worship."

memes.label: "Bowick Rectory"
memes.description: "Dr. Wortle's residence and the domestic centre of the novel, adjacent to Bowick School and the parish church, where the novel's most consequential private conversations occur."

memes.label: "Broughton"
memes.description: "Ecclesiastical seat of the Bishop of Broughton, a cathedral town central to the novel's investigation of the Peacocke scandal."

memes.label: "Broughton Gazette"
memes.description: "The local newspaper of Broughton that responds to the London-based Everybody's Business article about the Bowick scandal, amplifying the controversy at the diocesan level."

memes.label: "Carstairs"
memes.description: "Country estate of Earl Bracy, ancestral home of the Bracy family and setting for key social and romantic episodes in the novel."

memes.label: "Charity"
memes.description: "Christian charity is the novel's supreme moral value, invoked to justify Dr. Wortle's defence of the Peacockes and contrasted with the rigid moralism of their persecutors."

memes.label: "Church of England"
memes.description: "The established national church whose doctrines, hierarchy, and social role frame the clerical world of Dr. Wortle's School."

memes.label: "Class"
memes.description: "Social class shapes the novel's character dynamics, from the aristocratic pretensions of Mrs. Stantiloup to the professional standing of Dr. Wortle, and determines how the scandal is received across different social strata."

memes.label: "Clifford"
memes.description: "Collective reference to the two Clifford brothers, students at Bowick School whose parents withdrew them during the Peacocke scandal."

memes.label: "Conscience"
memes.description: "Conscience serves as the novel's highest moral authority, repeatedly invoked by Dr. Wortle to justify actions that defy social convention and ecclesiastical authority."

memes.label: "Courage"
memes.description: "Moral courage—the willingness to stand by one's convictions in the face of social pressure—is the defining virtue of Dr. Wortle and the quality most tested by the novel's central crisis."

memes.label: "Diocese of Broughton"
memes.description: "The ecclesiastical jurisdiction under the Bishop of Broughton that encompasses Bowick and represents institutional church authority in the novel."

memes.label: "Duty"
memes.description: "The concept of duty permeates the novel, governing the actions of clergy, teachers, husbands, and parents, and providing the moral framework within which characters justify their most consequential decisions."

memes.label: "The Earl of Brigstock"
memes.description: "The father of Mrs. Momson and a figure in the backstory of the Bracy family connection, mentioned as having married his daughter to the local squire."

memes.label: "Education"
memes.description: "The system and philosophy of education as depicted in Anthony Trollope's Dr. Wortle's School (1881), encompassing classical instruction, moral formation, and the social functions of elite schooling."

memes.label: "Eton"
memes.description: "The prestigious English public school where Dr. Wortle formerly taught before establishing his own school at Bowick, representing the educational ideal to which Bowick aspires."

memes.label: "Eton College"
memes.description: "Eton College, the prestigious English public school, serves as the aspirational destination for Bowick's students and as a marker of Dr. Wortle's own educational pedigree and social standing."

memes.label: "Everybody's Business"
memes.description: "A London weekly newspaper that publishes scandalous allegations about Dr. Wortle and the Peacockes, serving as a plot catalyst in the novel's second half and representing the destructive power of anonymous journalism."

memes.label: "Faith"
memes.description: "Faith operates on multiple levels in the novel—as religious conviction, as trust between individuals, and as the willingness to believe in another person's integrity without proof."

memes.label: "Ferdinand Lefroy"
memes.description: "The elder brother of Robert Lefroy and first husband of Mrs. Peacocke, presumed dead in the American Civil War; his survival and later confirmed death drive the central plot of the novel."

memes.label: "Gentleman"
memes.description: "The Victorian ideal of the gentleman—its social code, moral obligations, and role in defining character—permeates the moral landscape of Dr. Wortle's School."

memes.label: "Jack De Lawle"
memes.description: "The young son of Lady De Lawle, a pupil at Bowick School who appears in a memorable river-rescue incident early in the novel."

memes.label: "Dr. Jeffrey Wortle"
memes.description: "Headmaster of Bowick School and Rector of Bowick, protagonist of Anthony Trollope's Dr. Wortle's School (1881)"

memes.label: "Justice"
memes.description: "The novel explores justice as both a personal virtue and a social ideal, contrasting formal ecclesiastical justice with the deeper justice of individual conscience."

memes.label: "Lady Altamont"
memes.description: "A local aristocrat, the Marchioness of Altamont, who visits Bowick and whose titled presence adds lustre to the school's social standing."

memes.label: "Lady Anne Clifford"
memes.description: "Lady Anne Clifford, a widowed mother of two boys at Bowick School who supports Dr. Wortle through the Peacocke scandal and corresponds with him by letter."

memes.label: "Lady Bracy"
memes.description: "Wife of Earl Bracy and mother of Lord Carstairs, whose cordial reception of Mary Wortle at Carstairs helps validate the Wortle family's social standing."

memes.label: "Lady De Lawle"
memes.description: "Lady De Lawle, a local aristocrat and ally of Mrs. Stantiloup who pressures the Bishop to act against Dr. Wortle during the Peacocke scandal."

memes.label: "Lady Grogram"
memes.description: "A minor aristocrat, cousin and correspondent of Mrs. Stantiloup, who receives letters detailing the Peacocke scandal and its impact on Bowick School."

memes.label: "Lady Margaret"
memes.description: "A lady of fashion, rank, and wealth — wife of a county member and first cousin to Mrs. Stantiloup — who visits Mrs. Wortle to gather intelligence on the Peacocke scandal."

memes.label: "London"
memes.description: "The metropolis where Dr. Wortle seeks legal counsel and where Mr. Peacocke contemplates literary work, representing the world beyond Bowick's village confines."

memes.label: "Lord Bracy"
memes.description: "The Earl Bracy, father of Lord Carstairs, whose correspondence with Dr. Wortle and eventual acceptance of Mary Wortle as his daughter-in-law provides crucial aristocratic validation of the Wortle family."

memes.label: "Lord Carstairs"
memes.description: "Eldest son of Earl Bracy, a private pupil at Bowick School whose friendship with Mr. Peacocke and romantic interest in Mary Wortle drive key subplots."

memes.label: "Louisiana"
memes.description: "The American state where Ferdinand Lefroy claimed to own a plantation called Kilbrack, the purported family property of the Lefroy brothers."

memes.label: "Marriage"
memes.description: "The social and legal institution of marriage lies at the heart of the novel's central conflict, raising questions about validity, morality, and social standing in Victorian England."

memes.label: "Mary Wortle"
memes.description: "Mary Wortle, the only daughter of Dr. and Mrs. Wortle, whose romantic entanglement with Lord Carstairs forms a subplot of the novel."

memes.label: "Missouri"
memes.description: "The American state housing the college where Mr. Peacocke served as Vice-President and first met the woman who would become his wife, central to the Peacockes' transatlantic backstory."

memes.label: "Mr. Momson"
memes.description: "A parent of a Bowick pupil, one of the gentlemen drawn into Mrs. Stantiloup's campaign against Dr. Wortle's school."

memes.label: "Mr. Peacocke"
memes.description: "The assistant master at Dr. Wortle's school whose controversial marriage lies at the heart of the novel's central scandal."

memes.label: "Mr. Pearson"
memes.description: "The squire of Bowick, a local landowner whose listless manner contrasts with the more energetic clergymen of the parish, and who supports Dr. Wortle during the scandal."

memes.label: "Rev. Mr. Puddicombe"
memes.description: "The vicar of the next parish, a clergyman without a flaw who becomes Dr. Wortle's trusted confidant and moral advisor through the Peacocke scandal."

memes.label: "The Honourable Mr. Stantiloup"
memes.description: "Husband of the Honourable Mrs. Stantiloup, a minor figure whose son was removed from Bowick School after a dispute with Dr. Wortle."

memes.label: "Mr. Talbot"
memes.description: "Father of a Bowick School student and correspondent with Dr. Wortle about the Peacocke scandal, whose supportive letter helps stabilise the school's reputation."

memes.label: "Mrs. Jones"
memes.description: "The keeper of a modest boardinghouse in London where Robert Lefroy stays, and where Mr. Peacocke finds him during the search for proof of Ferdinand Lefroy's death."

memes.label: "Mrs. Momson"
memes.description: "Mrs. Momson, the squire's wife and mother of Augustus Momson, a student at Bowick School, who appears at social gatherings during the Peacocke scandal."

memes.label: "Mrs. Peacocke"
memes.description: "Wife of the assistant master at Bowick School, whose previous marriage in America becomes the central scandal of Trollope's novel."

memes.label: "Mrs. Rolland"
memes.description: "Wife of the Bishop of Broughton, who participates in the social conversations about the Peacocke scandal and helps shape public opinion about Dr. Wortle's school."

memes.label: "The Honourable Mrs. Stantiloup"
memes.description: "An aristocratic lady who leads a social campaign against Dr. Wortle's school over the Peacocke scandal."

memes.label: "Mrs. Talbot"
memes.description: "The mother of Jack Talbot, a student at Bowick School, and a connection of Mrs. Stantiloup through whom she exercises influence during the Peacocke scandal."

memes.label: "Mrs. Wortle"
memes.description: "Dr. Wortle's wife, a woman of strong moral conviction whose quiet loyalty and good sense anchor the Wortle household through the Peacocke scandal."

memes.label: "New Orleans"
memes.description: "The major Southern port city near the Lefroy plantation at Kilbrack, a point of transit and departure in the novel's transatlantic geography."

memes.label: "Ogden Junction"
memes.description: "The railway junction in Utah Territory where Ferdinand Lefroy allegedly died, a remote waypoint critical to resolving the central mystery of the Peacockes' marriage."

memes.label: "Oxford"
memes.description: "The ancient university city that represents scholarly ideals and Mr. Peacocke's former intellectual home, contrasting with the rural world of Bowick."

memes.label: "The Peacocke Scandal"
memes.description: "The revelation and unfolding of the irregular marriage of Mr. and Mrs. Peacocke, which threatens to destroy Dr. Wortle's school and tests the loyalties of every major character."

memes.label: "Peacocke's Journey to America"
memes.description: "Mr. Peacocke's journey to the United States to establish the truth about Ferdinand Lefroy's death is the novel's climactic quest, transforming the narrative from a domestic scandal into a transatlantic search for proof."

memes.label: "Peacocke's Return to Bowick"
memes.description: "Mr. Peacocke's return from America with proof of Ferdinand Lefroy's death marks the novel's resolution, restoring his reputation, validating his marriage, and vindicating Dr. Wortle's faith in him."

memes.label: "Reputation"
memes.description: "Reputation is the social currency at stake in the novel's central conflict, the fragile public asset that the scandal threatens and that characters must weigh against private conscience."

memes.label: "Robert Lefroy"
memes.description: "The younger brother of Mrs. Peacocke's first husband, an adventurer who arrives at Bowick to exploit the scandal around the Peacockes' marriage."

memes.label: "Saint Louis College"
memes.description: "The classical college in St. Louis, Missouri, where Mr. Peacocke served as vice-president and where he first met his future wife, setting in motion the chain of events that drives the novel's central plot."

memes.label: "San Francisco"
memes.description: "American city where Ferdinand Lefroy supposedly died of cholera, a claim central to the novel's mystery that requires transatlantic verification."

memes.label: "Scandal"
memes.description: "Scandal functions as the novel's narrative engine, driving the plot and exposing the gap between private morality and public reputation in Victorian society."

memes.label: "Shame"
memes.description: "Shame operates as an internal and external force in the novel, shaping character behaviour and revealing the gap between Victorian public morality and private feeling."

memes.label: "St. Louis"
memes.description: "American city where Mrs. Peacocke formerly lived, site of her first marriage to Ferdinand Lefroy and the College of St. Louis where he taught."

memes.label: "Jack Talbot"
memes.description: "A sharp-eyed young student at Bowick School who provides key eyewitness details to Robert Lefroy's arrival."

memes.label: "Truth"
memes.description: "The pursuit and disclosure of truth drives the novel's plot, as characters wrestle with whether, when, and to whom the truth should be told."
